course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
title: Multiplicity, Eigenspaces, and Eigenbases
sessionGoal: Students should be able to compare algebraic and geometric multiplicity and decide whether a matrix has enough eigenvectors for an eigenbasis.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start by defining algebraic multiplicity versus geometric multiplicity very simply.
Use Problem 1 to show repeated eigenvalue does not always mean enough eigenvectors.
Use Problem 2 to show repeated eigenvalue can still be okay.
Use Problem 3 as a fast conceptual eigenbasis decision problem.
sayThisFirst: Algebraic multiplicity counts how many times an eigenvalue appears in the characteristic polynomial.
Geometric multiplicity is the dimension of the eigenspace.
To build an eigenbasis for R^n, we need n linearly independent eigenvectors total.
Repeated eigenvalues are not automatically bad. They are bad only if the eigenspace is too small.
boardPlan: Write AM = factor count, GM = eigenspace dimension.
Write: diagonalizable/eigenbasis ⇔ total number of independent eigenvectors is n.
End with a quick eigenbasis construction from given eigenspaces.
keyFormulas: AM(λ) = multiplicity of λ as a root of the characteristic polynomial.
An n by n matrix has an eigenbasis if it has n linearly independent eigenvectors.
commonMistakes: Thinking AM and GM are always equal.
Thinking a repeated eigenvalue automatically means not diagonalizable.
Counting eigenvalues instead of counting independent eigenvectors.
Forgetting to include all eigenvectors when writing an eigenbasis.
notationWarnings: Use AM and GM only after defining them.
Write E_λ for eigenspace if that helps the board stay clean.
Be clear that an eigenbasis is a basis made entirely of eigenvectors.

problems.title: Repeated eigenvalue with too small an eigenspace
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \[A=\begin{bmatrix}2&1&0\\0&2&0\\0&0&3\end{bmatrix}.\] Find the algebraic and geometric multiplicities of each eigenvalue. Does \(A\) have an eigenbasis for \(\mathbb{R}^3\)?
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: this matrix has a repeated eigenvalue. We need to check the eigenspace size, not guess.

drawInstructions: Draw a table with columns λ, AM, GM.
Circle λ=2 because it is repeated.
Show that λ=2 only gives one eigenvector direction.

walkthrough.label: Read eigenvalues and algebraic multiplicities
walkthrough.latex: The matrix is triangular, so the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries: \(2,2,3\). Thus \(\lambda=2\) has AM \(2\), and \(\lambda=3\) has AM \(1\).

walkthrough.label: Find GM for λ=2
walkthrough.latex: \[A-2I=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&0\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(y=0\) and \(z=0\), with \(x\) free. So \(E_2=\operatorname{span}\{(1,0,0)\}\), and GM \(=1\).

walkthrough.label: Find GM for λ=3
walkthrough.latex: \[A-3I=\begin{bmatrix}-1&1&0\\0&-1&0\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(y=0\), then \(x=0\), with \(z\) free. So \(E_3=\operatorname{span}\{(0,0,1)\}\), and GM \(=1\).

walkthrough.label: Decide about eigenbasis
walkthrough.latex: Total independent eigenvectors: \(1+1=2\). For \(\mathbb{R}^3\), we need \(3\). So there is no eigenbasis.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: \[\lambda=2:\ AM=2,\ GM=1;\qquad \lambda=3:\ AM=1,\ GM=1.\] No eigenbasis for \(\mathbb{R}^3\).
commonMistakes: Saying λ=2 gives two eigenvectors just because it appears twice.
Only counting the number of distinct eigenvalues.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 5 for multiplicity and eigenspaces.

problems.title: Repeated eigenvalue that still gives enough eigenvectors
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \[B=\begin{bmatrix}4&0&0\\0&4&1\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}.\] Decide whether \(B\) has an eigenbasis for \(\mathbb{R}^3\). If it does, give one.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: repeated eigenvalues are not automatically bad. We need to count eigenspace dimensions.

drawInstructions: Make a table of eigenvalues and eigenspace bases.
Emphasize that λ=4 gives two directions.

walkthrough.label: Find eigenvalues
walkthrough.latex: Because \(B\) is triangular, the eigenvalues are \(4,4,1\).

walkthrough.label: Find eigenspace for λ=4
walkthrough.latex: \[B-4I=\begin{bmatrix}0&0&0\\0&0&1\\0&0&-3\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(z=0\), with \(x\) and \(y\) free. So \[E_4=\operatorname{span}\{(1,0,0),(0,1,0)\}.\]

walkthrough.label: Find eigenspace for λ=1
walkthrough.latex: \[B-I=\begin{bmatrix}3&0&0\\0&3&1\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(x=0\) and \(3y+z=0\). Let \(y=1\), so \(z=-3\). Thus \[E_1=\operatorname{span}\{(0,1,-3)\}.\]

walkthrough.label: Count eigenvectors
walkthrough.latex: We have \(2\) independent eigenvectors from \(E_4\) and \(1\) from \(E_1\), for \(3\) total. So \(B\) has an eigenbasis.

commonMistakes: Rejecting the matrix only because λ=4 is repeated.
Forgetting that the eigenspace for λ=4 is two-dimensional.
Choosing the zero vector as an eigenvector.

problems.title: Use given eigenspaces to decide if an eigenbasis exists
problems.studentPromptLatex: Suppose \(A\) is a \(3\times3\) matrix with characteristic polynomial \((\lambda-2)^2(\lambda+1)\). Also suppose \[E_2=\operatorname{span}\{(1,0,1),(0,1,-1)\},\qquad E_{-1}=\operatorname{span}\{(1,1,0)\}.\] Does \(A\) have an eigenbasis for \(\mathbb{R}^3\)? If yes, give one.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: this problem gives the eigenspaces, so do not row reduce. Just count independent eigenvectors.

drawInstructions: Draw a table: λ, AM, eigenspace basis, GM.
Write λ=2 has AM 2 and GM 2.
Write λ=-1 has AM 1 and GM 1.
Combine the eigenspace bases into one eigenbasis.

walkthrough.label: Find AM from the characteristic polynomial
walkthrough.latex: The factor \((\lambda-2)^2\) means \(\lambda=2\) has AM \(2\). The factor \((\lambda+1)\) means \(\lambda=-1\) has AM \(1\).

walkthrough.label: Find GM from the eigenspaces
walkthrough.latex: The eigenspace \(E_2\) has two basis vectors, so GM for \(\lambda=2\) is \(2\). The eigenspace \(E_{-1}\) has one basis vector, so GM for \(\lambda=-1\) is \(1\).

walkthrough.label: Count total eigenvectors
walkthrough.latex: Total independent eigenvectors: \(2+1=3\). Since this is a \(3\times3\) matrix, that is enough for an eigenbasis.

walkthrough.label: Write the eigenbasis
walkthrough.latex: One eigenbasis is the union of the eigenspace bases: \[\{(1,0,1),(0,1,-1),(1,1,0)\}.\]

commonMistakes: Row reducing even though the eigenspaces are already given.
Forgetting to include the eigenvector for λ=-1.
Thinking the repeated eigenvalue λ=2 is automatically a problem.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 5 for AM, GM, and eigenbases.

wrapUpQuestions: What is the difference between AM and GM?
How many independent eigenvectors do we need for an eigenbasis of R^3?
Can a repeated eigenvalue still be okay?

linksToWebsite.note: Practice Lab Section(s) 5 for more practice.

qualityControlNotes: Checked A example: λ=2 has GM 1, λ=3 has GM 1.
Checked B example: λ=4 eigenspace has dimension 2 and λ=1 has eigenvector (0,1,-3).
Checked conceptual eigenbasis problem totals 3 eigenvectors.
reviewSummary: Session 12 is about counting eigenvector directions, not just finding eigenvalues.
